coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots and CuS microflowers anchored rGO composite counter electrode for the enhanced performance of quantum dot sensitized solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Monika, S.
Mahalakshmi, M.
Subha, N.
Pandian, M. Senthil
Ramasamy, P.
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQD) anchored on reduced graphene oxide (rGO) sheets were synthesized by hydrothermal method. CuS and GQD anchored rGO sheet (GRGS/CuS) composite was prepared by simple spin coating and thermal sulfidation techniques and used as counter electrode (CE) material for CdS quantum dot sensitized solar cell (QDSSC). The morphological studies of GRGS/CuS composite revealed the presence of spherical graphene quantum dots and microflower structured CuS on rGO sheet matrix. This microflower morphology provided more reactive sites for the electrolyte reduction and GQD/rGO in the composite acted as an effective conducting matrix to transport the charge carriers. The synergistic effect of GQD/rGO and CuS in the composite resulted in reduced charge transfer resistance and multi fold times increased the power conversion efficiency of GRGS/CuS composite than the bare counter electrodes. This work has opened up new opportunities to use GRGS/CuS composite material as a promising counter electrode, since it has the potential to enhance the performance of solar cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dot sensitized solar cells
CdS quantum dots
Graphene quantum dots
Reduced graphene oxide
Composite counter electrode

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of quantum dot sensitized photoanodes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD pattern of synthesized graphene oxide and GRGS (b) XRD pattern of GRGS/CuS composite counter electrode.
(a, b) - HRSEM images of bare CuS CE in different magnification.
(a) - HRSEM image of GRGS/CuS composite, (b&C) HRTEM images of GRGS/CuS and lattice fringes of CuS, rGO and GQD (d) lattice fringes of CuS with the hexagonal cavity, (inset) is the SEAD pattern.
(a) - XPS survey spectra of GRGS/CuS counter electrode (b) C1s spectrum (c) O1s (d) Cu2p spectrum (e) S2p spectrum.
(a) Raman spectra of GRGS (b) GRGS/CuS composite, CuS (inset).
(a) Nyquist impedance plots (b) Tafel polarization of CdS QDSSCs assembled with CuS and GRGS and GRGS/CuS composite CE.
Current density - voltage curves of CdS QDSSCs assembled with bare GRGS, CuS and GRGS/CuS counter electrodes.
Stability evaluation of J-V curves (a) VOC (b) JSC (C) FF (d) PCE (e) Normalized PCE for the QDSSCs with CuS, GRGS and GRGS/CuS composite counter electrodes.
The atomic percentage of elements (at.%) obtained from XPS spectra of GRGS/CuS counter electrode.
Impedance parameters and photovoltaic parameters of CdS QDSSCs assembled with CuS, GRGS and GRGS/CuS Counter Electrodes.
Photovoltaic parameters comparison with previously reported results of CdS quantum dot sensitized solar cells.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots and CuS microflowers anchored rGO composite counter electrode for the enhanced performance of quantum dot sensitized solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Sri Sivasubramaniya Nadar College of Engineering, Kalavakkam, Chennai 603110, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sri Sivasubramaniya Nadar College of Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Sri Sivasubramaniya Nadar College of Engineering, Kalavakkam, Chennai 603110, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: SSN Research Centre, Sri Sivasubramaniya Nadar College of Engineering, Kalavakkam, Chennai 603110, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sri Sivasubramaniya Nadar College of Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: SSN Research Centre, Sri Sivasubramaniya Nadar College of Engineering, Kalavakkam, Chennai 603110, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQD) anchored on reduced graphene oxide (rGO) sheets were synthesized by hydrothermal method. CuS and GQD anchored rGO sheet (GRGS/CuS) composite was prepared by simple spin coating and thermal sulfidation techniques and used as counter electrode (CE) material for CdS quantum dot sensitized solar cell (QDSSC). The morphological studies of GRGS/CuS composite revealed the presence of spherical graphene quantum dots and microflower structured CuS on rGO sheet matrix. This microflower morphology provided more reactive sites for the electrolyte reduction and GQD/rGO in the composite acted as an effective conducting matrix to transport the charge carriers. The synergistic effect of GQD/rGO and CuS in the composite resulted in reduced charge transfer resistance and multi fold times increased the power conversion efficiency of GRGS/CuS composite than the bare counter electrodes. This work has opened up new opportunities to use GRGS/CuS composite material as a promising counter electrode, since it has the potential to enhance the performance of solar cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Increasing demand for renewable energy resources has accelerated intensive research in various nanostructured materials for solar cell applications. Quantum dot-sensitized solar cells (QDSSCs) are arising as a promising solar cell technology owing to their striking optoelectronic features as well as their facile fabrication and low-cost availability [1]. Nevertheless, harnessing the full potential of QDSSC is challenging because of the impediments in choosing suitable electrode materials and fabrication techniques. Hence, various nanostructured architectures are adapted and investigated as electrode materials to increase the power conversion efficiency of solar cells. Specifically, graphene-based materials have gained considerable attention since the Nobel Prize-winning research on graphene by Geim and Novoselov [2]. The discovery of graphene with its exceptional properties such as high thermal stability, superior electrical conductivity, high mobility and catalytic activity, fostered the development of efficient energy-related devices. Among various type of graphene nanostructures, graphene quantum dots (GQD) has ignited a tremendous amount of research interest due to their versatile optical and electronic properties with quantum confinement and edge effects. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are graphene sheets with less than 100 nm of lateral size [3]. GQDs have much smaller sizes, while retaining the graphitic nature, unique electron transportation properties and large surface area [4]. Because of their peculiar properties, GQDs are well suited for solar cell applications. Hitherto, GQDs have been reported as hole transport layer (HTL) in organic solar cells [5], counter electrode and photoanode for DSSC [6,7], electron transport layer in perovskite solar cells [8] and others [9,10]. GQD was also employed as photosensitizer in QDSSC but reported with less efficiency [11], which might be due to the poor light absorption. Since the absorption band of GQD mainly lies in UV region (~320 nm) hence resulted in deficient exciton generation. Therefore, the insufficiency of preferred band gap impounds the suitability of graphene as a photosensitizer for QDSSCs [12]. However, graphene has an excellent conductive network which can generate an efficient charge transfer due to its available delocalized electrons and high electron mobility. Hence, GQD can be well utilized as counter electrodes to effectively transfer electrons from the external circuit towards the reduction of oxidized electrolyte. CuS widely used counter electrode for QDSSCs has excellent electrocatalytic activity for the redox reaction, its mechanical instability is inevitable in the polysulfide electrolyte [13]. Thus, composite counter electrodes can be employed to overcome the shortcomings of conventional counter electrodes. Some of the reported CuS based composite counter electrodes are CuS/MWCNT composite [14] Copper sulfide (CuxS)/reduced graphene oxide (RGO) [15], graphitic C3N4 (g-C3N4) nanosheets and CuS nanoparticles for solar cell applications [16].

In the present work, the novel graphene quantum dots anchored reduced graphene oxide sheets (GRGS) were synthesized by hydrothermal method. Then GRGS/CuS composite was employed as counter electrode for QDSSC application. GRGS/CuS composite exhibited a better performance than the bare counter electrodes due to the complementary effects of graphene quantum dots and CuS microflowers anchored on reduced graphene oxide sheets, which showed the excellent charge transfer efficiency and rapid electrolyte reduction respectively. Hence, the GRGS/CuS composite counter electrode has promising future in solar cell applications.

Titanium (IV) isopropoxide (Sigma-Aldrich), Fluorine doped tin oxide (FTO) conducting glass substrate (Sigma-Aldrich), Ethylcellulose (Sigma-Aldrich), α-terpineol (Alfa-Aesar), Cadmium nitrate tetrahydrate (Merck), Sodium sulfide flakes nonahydrate (RANKEM) were used as cadmium and sulfur source materials respectively. Copper II nitrate trihydrate (Merck), Graphite powder (Sigma-Aldrich), Sulfur (Merck), Potassium chloride (Merck) Absolute ethanol (Merck) and double distilled water were used for the synthesis and preparation of electrodes.

TiO2 nanoparticles were synthesized as reported in our earlier work [17] and were made into a paste by using ethyl cellulose, α-terpineol and ethanol. The paste was coated on cleaned FTO substrates by the doctor blade method. Then the substrates were annealed at 450 °C for half an hour. The thickness of the TiO2 films was measured using the Bruker thickness profilometer, the measured thickness was approximately 26 μm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of quantum dot sensitized photoanodes
ce:section.ce:para: TiO2 electrodes were coated with CdS sensitizer by successive ionic layer adsorption and reaction (SILAR) method. This method consists of dipping and rinsing with precursor and solvent respectively. For CdS QDs deposition, TiO2 electrodes were introduced in 0.05 M Cd(NO3)2.4H2O dissolved in water and 0.05 M Na2S in water as Cd2+ and S2− sources respectively. The electrodes were rinsed with water every time to remove excess precursors that were loosely adsorbed, before immersing the electrodes alternatively in the respective precursors.

GO was synthesized by modified hummers method using graphite [17] and GQD anchored rGO was prepared via one-pot hydrothermal method using the synthesized GO powder [18]. 0.5 g of as-prepared GO was added into the 16 ml nitric acid aqueous solution and refluxed for 24 h at 70 °C. Once it cooled down, the suspension was sonicated and NaOH was added until the pH reached 7. After centrifugation and filtration, the mixture was transferred to autoclave and kept in oven for 24 h at 200 °C. The resultant mixture of GQD dispersed supernatant solution and black precipitate of rGO was obtained [19]. Finally, the product was dried at 100 °C and obtained black powder of graphene quantum dots anchored reduced graphene oxide sheets (GRGS) was used for counter electrode preparation.

For the preparation of GRGS/CuS CE, a simple method was followed with slight modification from the literature [20]. GRGS/Cu thin films were first prepared by spin coating the dispersion, consisting of the required amount of synthesized GRGS powder, Cu(NO3)2.3H2O and ethyl cellulose in ethanol on cleaned FTO substrate. The coating cycles were optimized as two at 2000 rpm. After coating, the substrates were dried using a hot plate at 60 °C for 15 min. This thin film was taken for sulfidation treatment by immersing it into an aqueous solution of 0.1 M Na2S at 60 °C for 30 min. For comparison, bare CuS and GRGS films were also prepared by spin coating with the respective solution followed by sulfidation. The thickness of the GRGS/CuS film was approximately 14 μm.

QDSSCs were assembled with CdS sensitized TiO2 films as the photoanodes and different CEs such as CuS, GRGS and GRGS/CuS CEs with an active area of 0.25 cm2. The cells were filled with redox liquid electrolyte (0.5 M Na2S, 2 M Sulfur and 0.2 M KCl in DI water) and then tested under ~100 mW cm−2 illumination. The active area of the QDSSCs was 0.25 cm2.

The XRD patterns of the synthesized GO, GRGS and GRGS/CuS obtained were shown in Fig. 1 . In the XRD pattern, a sharp peak at 2θ = 10.5° with larger interplanar d-spacing of 0.84 nm corresponds to the (001) plane of graphene oxide [21]. The broader diffraction peak at 2θ = 25° and 43° correspond to the (002) and (1120) planes with interplanar d-spacing of 0.36 and 0.21 nm respectively. The obtained d spacing values are matching with the interlayer spacing of both GQD and reduced graphene oxide sheets [22]. The calculated crystallite size of ~5 nm for the diffraction peak confirmed graphene quantum dots, it is expected that the diffraction peak of rGO was merged within the GQD peak. The d-spacing value is influenced by the oxygen functionalities present on the basal plane of the graphene sheet. The d-spacing value of GQD (0.36 nm) greater than the bulk graphite (0.33 nm) revealed the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups in the synthesized material [23,24]. However, this is smaller than the d-spacing value of graphene oxide (0.84 nm), which could be related to the size and edge effects of GQDs, because oxidation might take place only on the edges of GQDs due to their very small size [22]. The XRD pattern of GRGS/CuS composite confirmed the presence of graphene and CuS in the composite, the low peak intensity of CuS was due to the dominant graphene content. The peaks around 29.2, 32.2, 47.6 correspond to the (102), (006) and (110) planes of covellite CuS (PDF 85–0620) with d-spacing values 0.31 nm, 0.28 nm, 0.22 nm respectively confirmed the presence of CuS [25]. Hence the XRD results revealed the presence of CuS, GQD and rGO in the synthesized composite material.

The morphologies of bare CuS and GRGS/CuS composite were investigated by HRSEM characterization. The morphology of bare CuS counter electrode showed interlinked nanoflakes of CuS as displayed in Fig. 2 (a, b). The HRSEM image of GRGS/CuS composite shows microflowers of CuS and GQD decorated rGO sheet in Fig. 3(a). This confirmed that the morphology of CuS changed into a flower-like structure, while depositing CuS along with graphene. The hierarchical flower-like structure of CuS with an average diameter of about 3–4 μm size were formed by the spherical aggregation of a large number of thin nanoflakes of CuS [26]. It was reported that the spherical aggregation of CuS nanoflakes could be due to the interaction of oxygen-containing functional groups of graphene with the nuclei of CuS crystals, which led to the formation of CuS microflowers on graphene sheets. This phenomenon also increased the nucleation sites of CuS crystals and the adhesion of CuS microflowers on the surface of rGO sheets [27]. The peculiar hexagonal type lattice fringes in Fig. 3(d) with a hexagonal cavity in the center indicated the hollow interior of CuS hexagonal nanoflakes, which were intertwined to result in a hierarchical flower-like structure of CuS. This architecture led to the occurrence of a larger surface area for electrolyte diffusion and also served as an in-plane charge transporter during the electrochemical reactions [28,29].

The HRTEM image in Fig. 3(b) revealed that more homogeneous spherical-shaped GQDs were obtained with an average size of ~4 to 6 nm. Fig. 3(c) showed GQD and CuS inhabited rGO sheet and confirmed the formation of GRGS/CuS composite material. The lattice fringes with the d-spacing of approximately 0.35 and 0.24 nm can be ascribed to (002) and (1120) planes of graphene quantum dots and rGO respectively. Furthermore, the lattice fringes observed with d-spacing values of 0.28 and 0.19 nm were assigned to hexagonal covellite CuS [30]. The lattice fringes of CuS with different orientations indicated that CuS was probably proceeding to form a single crystalline structure, which was also confirmed by the quasi-single-crystal feature from the SEAD pattern and lattice fringes of CuS with the hexagonal cavity as shown in Fig. 3(d) [31].

The chemical structure of GRGS/CuS composite was further confirmed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. Fig. 4(a) shows the XPS survey spectra of the as-prepared GRGS/CuS composite counter electrode. The XPS results showed a predominant graphitic C1s peak at 285.86 eV and a peak of O1s at 532.54 eV and O1s peak along with Cu2p peak and S2p peak belong to GRGS and CuS respectively. The high resolution C1s peaks of GQD in Fig. 4(b) was deconvoluted into several peaks corresponding to sp2 C (CC/CC) at 284.6 eV, sp3 C (COH) at 285.8 eV, CO at 287.81 eV and OCO at 288.96 eV [32,33]. XPS spectrum of O1s in Fig. 4(c) inferred that many different forms of oxygen exist in the system and confirmed the presence of CO at 531.3 eV, COH at 532.1 eV and OCOH (535.1 eV) bonds. These results suggested that the functionalization of graphene sheets with oxygen-containing functional groups such as hydroxyl and carbonyl took place during the preparation process, which was also supported by XRD and RAMAN results [34,35]. The two strong peaks in the Cu2p core level spectrum in Fig. 4(d) with binding energies at 931.8 eV and 951.8 eV were observed due to the Cu2p3/2 and 2p1/2 of the Cu2p phase respectively. The two weak shake-up satellite peaks at 943.3 and 962.7 eV on the high binding energy side of the Cu2p core level peaks showed the presence of mixed Cu2+ and Cu+ vacancies in CuS lattice [36,37]. Further, in Fig. 4(e), the XPS core-level spectra of S2p were distinguished by three typical peaks revealing that there were distinct S sites in CuS. S2− species were confirmed by the peaks at binding energies 161.8 and 162.5 eV containing S2p3/2 and S2p1/2 components respectively. The distinctive peak at 168.6 eV corresponds to the presence of SO bond [38]. The presence of SO bond stipulates, the chemisorption of CuS on rGO and FTO through –OH group of rGO and surface O2− of FTO respectively. The presence of CuS and graphene in GRGS/CuS composite counter electrode material was confirmed by the XPS results. The atomic percentage (at.%) of elements obtained from XPS spectra of GRGS/CuS counter electrode is given in Table 1 .

The Raman spectrum of GRGS in the spectral range 100–3300 cm−1 was obtained as shown, where the peaks around 1348 cm−1 and 1591 cm−1 represent the D and G bands of the disordered nature and graphitic nature of the carbon material respectively. The G band peak is related to E2g phonon at the Brillouin zone observed for sp2 carbon domains and the D band is attributed to the breathing mode of k-point phonons of A1g symmetry, which is assigned to the local defects and disorder at the edges of graphene. The relative intensity of the D over G band, ID/IG = 0.91 suggested the presence of defects due to the oxygen-containing functional groups at the edges and basal planes of the constituent graphene sheets and GQDs [39–41]. The broad peaks centered at 2690 and 2911 cm−1 belong to the 2D and D + G bands respectively, corresponding to combination modes/overtones arising from the relaxation in selection rules caused by phonon scattering at boundaries and defects in GQDs [42–44]. Fig. 5(b) inset shows the Raman spectrum of CuS with a sharp peak located at 475 cm−1, which belongs to the A1g mode of CuS. A less prominent peak at 266 cm−1 was due to the lattice SS stretching vibration mode of CuS. This confirmed the presence of covellite CuS structure [45]. The Raman spectrum of GRGS/CuS composite in Fig. 5(b) confirmed the successful anchoring of GQD and CuS on rGO sheets by exhibiting the peak for CuS along with D, G bands and 2D, D+G bands of graphene structure.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and Tafel polarization measurements were conducted to evaluate the electrochemical properties of CdS QDSSCs fabricated with CuS, GRGS and GRGS/CuS CEs. Fig. 6a, shows the Nyquist plots of the QDSSCs and inset shows the equivalent circuit for fitting and the electrochemical and photovoltaic parameters are displayed in Table 2 . From the Nyquist plot, the charge transfer resistance (Rct) of GRGS/CuS was obtained as 8.5 Ω cm2 , while that of bare CuS and GRGS was 11.7 Ω cm2 and 221 Ω cm2 respectively. The large charge transfer resistance of bare GRGS counter electrode revealed its poor electrocatalytic activity in polysulfide electrolyte, which was due to the absence of CuS active sites to transfer the electrons for electrolyte reduction. This is evident from the highly suppressed Rct value for GRGS/CuS. It is speculated that the conductivity of bare graphene was lowered due to the strong adsorption of electrolyte S2− ions, leading to its poor performance. CuS nanoparticles chemisorbed on GQD and rGO sheet played crucial role in transporting electrons to the reduction of oxidized species of polysulfide electrolyte and enhanced the catalytic activity. The unique linear electronic dispersion near the K-point Brillouin zone of graphene enables fast dissociation of electrons from the injection site, making the electrons move rapidly along the graphene sheet [46]. In the case of composite, graphene firmly holds CuS nanoparticles and also acts as a conducting matrix to transport the charge carriers, whereas, CuS provides active sites for the reduction of electrolytes.

Tafel polarization analysis was studied to further confirm the electrocatalytic activity of CEs. Fig. 6b, shows the Tafel curves plotted with logarithmic current density as a function of the applied potential. The ability of the CEs to reduce the electrolyte ions can be evaluated by the exchange current density J0, which can be estimated from the intersection of tangent line from cathodic branch and the equilibrium potential line [47]. The J0 values of the CEs were in the following order, GRGS/CuS > CuS > GRGS, this correlates well with the Rct values (GRGS/CuS < CuS < GRGS) obtained from EIS studies, where Rct is inversely proportional to J0 as given in following equation, J0 = RT/nFRct, where R is the universal gas constant, T is the absolute temperature, n is the number of electrons contributing to the charge transfer at the interface and F is the Faraday's constant. Consequently, a higher J0 substantially implies smaller charge transfer resistance and the superior electrocatalytic activity of the composite CE. The cathodic branch at high current density is known as limiting diffusion current density (Jlim), which is associated with the diffusivity of the redox couple and catalytic behaviour of the CE [48]. The Jlim values of the CEs was obtained as in the following sequence, GRGS/CuS > CuS > GRGS, indicating that CuS and its composite GRGS/CuS possess high diffusion velocity for the reduction of electrolyte compared to the bare GRGS. Hence, our experimental results also confirmed the suitability of CuS in polysulfide electrolyte. The results obtained from EIS and Tafel polarization plots were consistent with each other. Hence it is confirmed that the co-existence of graphene and CuS have complementary effects in enhancing the performance of GRGS/CuS composite CE with enhanced current density and decreased charge transfer resistance.

The J–V characteristics of CuS, GRGS, GRGS/CuS CEs were measured with CdS quantum dot sensitized TiO2 photoanode displayed in Fig. 7 . The open-circuit voltage (Voc) is determined by the quasi-fermi level of TiO2 and redox potential of electrolyte, hence Voc values are nearly the same with all QDSSCs. There was a prominent increase in short-circuit current density (JSC) for the cell fabricated with GRGS/CuS CE as 7.29 mA/cm2 than that of cells with CuS (2.56 mA/cm2) and GRGS (1.99 mA/cm2) which was attributed to the reduced charge transfer resistance of the composite. QDSSC fabricated with GRGS/CuS CE possesses an overall efficiency (η) of 1.9%, which was higher than that of bare CuS (0.38%) and GRGS CEs (0.26%). The higher conductivity and faster charge transfer of GRGS/CuS CE improved the fill factor (FF) and short-circuit current density (Jsc) which resulted in increased power conversion efficiency. This enhancement in performance was accredited to the unique architecture of CuS microflowers and graphene quantum dots anchored rGO sheet structure which provided an excellent pathway for electron transport. It is known that the photovoltaic performance of GRGS/CuS composite is superior to the reported results based on various counter electrodes for CdS QDSSC shown in Table 3 .

CuS and graphene quantum dots anchored reduced graphene oxide sheets (GRGS/CuS) composite counter electrode showed significant improvement in PCE of CdS QDSSCs. This was due to the combined effect of CuS microflowers and GQD anchored rGO sheet structure of the counter electrode. The hierarchical flower-like structure of CuS and spherical GQD anchored rGO sheet was revealed by morphological analysis. This combination of architecture facilitated an effective charge transfer network for the transfer of charge carriers at the interface of the CE and polysulfide redox electrolyte, which also ensures enough surface area for electrolyte ion diffusion during the electrochemical reactions. The presence of SO bond in XPS indicated that oxygen-containing groups in rGO and surface oxygen of FTO promoted the chemical adsorption of CuS on graphene surface as well as on the FTO. The oxygen functional group of rGO also established strong chemisorption by hydrogen bonding with the surface O2− or F− ions of fluorinated tin oxide (FTO). These phenomena provided a better adhesion of composite on FTO and hence promoted electron collection and transportation with high current density.

The stability of the counter electrodes with CdS QDSSC was investigated by performing J-V characteristics under continuous illumination. The obtained photovoltaic parameters of the cells are shown in Fig. 8 revealed the promising stability of the device with composite counter electrode during the time mentioned. The device fabricated with GRGS/CuS composite counter electrode retained 82% of its initial PCE, while the devices with bare CuS and GRGS counter electrodes retained 53% and 36% from their initial PCE values respectively after various J-V cycles. From the results, it is confirmed that the composite CE showed improved performance stability, since graphene has provided better adhesion and acted as a support matrix for CuS nanoparticles. The stability of the device decreases with time due to degradation of the sensitizer and photoanode by the infiltration of liquid electrolyte, which decreases the durability of QDSSCs. By working on the issues like infiltration, leakage and volatilization of liquid electrolyte, the photo-stability of the QDSSCs can be further increased.

We developed novel CuS microflowers and GQD anchored reduced graphene oxide sheets (GRGS/CuS CE) composite as counter electrode for CdS QDSSC. The morphological analysis confirmed that incorporating graphene with CuS led to the formation of microflower like structure of CuS. This architecture established a larger surface area to accommodate more CuS reactive sites for electrolyte reduction with high charge transfer efficiency. The chemisorption between FTO, rGO and CuS facilitated better adhesion of the composite on FTO, which improved electrocatalytic activity of the composite. A large number of CuS active sites for electrolyte reduction and excellent conductivity of graphene framework in the GRGS/CuS composite synergistically helped to reduce the charge transfer resistance of the composite counter electrode with increased current density and to enhance the photovoltaic performance than bare CuS and GRGS CEs. These encouraging results indicate that the novel GRGS/CuS composite has a brilliant future and can also be explored with other metal sulfides as a suitable counter electrode to design high-efficiency solar cells.

S. Monika: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. M. Mahalakshmi: Investigation, Supervision. N. Subha: Resources. M. Senthil Pandian: Validation. P. Ramasamy: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge SSN Institutions for the financial support to pursue the research work.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: 1. Z. Pan, H. Rao, I. Mora-Seró, J. Bisquert, X. Zhong, Quantum dot sensitized solar cells. Chem. Soc. Rev., 47(20) (2018) 7659-7702.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A GQD-based composite film as photon down-converter in CNT/Si solar cells
